content:
First, recall that by the Euclid–Euler theorem every even perfect number has the form
  \(2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\)
with \(2^p-1\) prime.  No odd perfect numbers are known (and are widely believed not to exist).

We seek \(n>0\) such that
  \(n^n+1=2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\).

1.  If \(n\) is even then \(n^n\) is even, so \(n^n+1\) is odd.  Thus it would have to be an odd perfect number, which is not known to exist.  So no even \(n\) work.

2.  If \(n\) is odd then \(n^n+1\) is even, so it might equal an even perfect number
     \(2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\).

3.  Check small odd \(n\):

   -  \(n=1\):  \(1^1+1=2\), not perfect.
   -  \(n=3\):  \(3^3+1=28=2^2\cdot7\), and \(7=2^3-1\) is prime.  Indeed \(28\) is perfect.
   -  \(n=5\):  \(5^5+1=3126\), not of the form \(2^{p-1}(2^p-1)\) with \(2^p-1\) prime.
   -  \(n\ge7\):  \(n^n+1\) grows too quickly to match the next perfect numbers \(\,496,8128,33550336,\dots\)

Hence the only solution is 
\[
\boxed{3}\,.
\]

2.  If \(n\) is odd then \(n^n+1\) is even, so it might equal an even perfect number

   -  \(n=3\):  \(3^3+1=28=2^2\cdot7\), and \(7=2^3-1\) is prime.  Indeed \(28\) is perfect.
